network.overall_strategy: The network follows the literature consensus that prebiotic chemistry robustly generates racemic alanine through multiple convergent routes centered on HCN, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, and alpha-aminopropionitrile, while L-alanine is most plausibly obtained only later through terrestrial, network-level symmetry breaking rather than a single direct abiotic enantioselective synthesis. Accordingly, the graph builds a connected Hydrothermal ↔ Surface → Biochemical network: hydrothermal vent chemistry generates pyruvate and carbonyl feedstocks; surface environments generate HCN, acetaldehyde, aminonitriles, and HCN oligomer pools; multiple racemic alanine-forming routes converge on DL-alanine; and a final explicitly speculative but literature-motivated biochemical selection module converts a DL-alanine pool into L-enriched alanine through indirect symmetry breaking associated with RNA/CISS-enabled homochiral selection and autocatalytic network effects.

reactions.name: Classic Miller-Urey spark generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.temperature: ambient to warm, spark-heated local discharge; 1 week experiment
conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge with mineral concentration surfaces
conditions.other: reducing gas mixture CH4/NH3/H2/H2O; products dissolve into aqueous phase

reactions.mechanism: Spark discharge converts reduced atmospheric gases into nitriles and carbonyls; HCN and acetaldehyde formed in situ then accumulate in water.
reactions.reasoning: This abstracts the documented Miller-Urey precursor-generating regime in which HCN and acetaldehyde are produced from CH4/NH3/H2/H2O and subsequently feed Strecker chemistry. Mineral agents are included as plausible concentrating shoreline photocatalytic surfaces to satisfy network environmental constraints, but the core chemistry is the discharge chemistry of the experiment (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Volcanic H2S-enhanced spark generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.temperature: ambient to warm, spark-heated local discharge; 1 week experiment
conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge in H2S-containing volcanic gas mixture
conditions.other: volcanic apparatus analogue; alanine precursor formation enhanced relative to classic apparatus

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in sulfur-bearing gas mixtures generates reactive nitriles and aldehydes with higher amino-acid precursor productivity.
reactions.reasoning: The H2S-containing Miller volcanic variant yielded higher alanine-related product inventories than the classic experiment; the network uses it as an independent convergent source of HCN and acetaldehyde feeding Strecker branches (Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fe/Ni-mediated pyruvate formation from CO2

conditions.catalyst: Fe/Ni-like mineral surfaces represented by greigite and magnetite
conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent to vent-margin conditions; reducing environment

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated CO2 reduction and C-C bond construction produce alpha-keto acid intermediates including pyruvate under reducing hydrothermal conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a documented prebiotic branch-point produced under Fe/Ni-mediated CO2 reduction and reverse-TCA-like chemistry. This node connects hydrothermal carbon fixation to multiple alanine-forming routes, though upstream mechanism remains more condition-sensitive than alanine-forming steps themselves (Muchowska et al., 2019; Beyazay et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Pyrite-coupled reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: iron-sulfur minerals with pyrite-forming redox coupling
conditions.other: linked to oxidative pyrite formation; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes reductive amination with ammonia using reducing equivalents generated during FeS/H2S to FeS2 conversion, yielding racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: This directly follows the pyrite-driven reductive amination literature and is among the most explicit hydrothermal alanine-producing routes (Hafenbradl et al., 1995).

reactions.name: Mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: mixed-valence Fe(II)/Fe(III) mineral phases, optimum near 1:1 ratio

reactions.mechanism: Mixed-valence iron minerals transfer electrons to promote reductive amination of pyruvate by ammonia, producing racemic alanine under alkaline conditions.
reactions.reasoning: This captures the alkaline vent-consistent iron-mediated pyruvate-to-alanine route and the importance of mixed Fe(II):Fe(III) mineral redox states (Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Ni/H2-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.other: pH 7-8; 30 mM pyruvate; mild prebiotic catalytic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Nickel catalyzes hydrogen-driven reductive amination of pyruvate with ammonium/ammonia to alanine under mild aqueous conditions.
reactions.reasoning: This modern prebiotic analogue closely parallels biosynthetic alanine formation and is one of the best-defined recent alanine-forming reactions, though it yields racemic alanine rather than L-alanine (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker condensation to alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions at acidic or basic pH; imine forms then cyanide addition occurs

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde condenses with ammonia to ethanimine, followed by cyanide addition to produce alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical Strecker route to alanine precursor formation and the dominant literature-supported route from acetaldehyde to alanine chemistry (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Sequential hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to alaninamide and DL-alanine

conditions.other: stepwise nitrile hydrolysis through amide intermediate under mild aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolyzes first to alaninamide and then to racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The aminonitrile-to-amide-to-amino-acid sequence is the established downstream phase of Strecker alanine synthesis (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker formation of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: UV-driven cyanosulfidic chemistry with phosphate present
conditions.other: neutral pH; phosphoro-Strecker context; literature reports ~70% aminonitrile yield from acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Under cyanosulfidic conditions, acetaldehyde is converted efficiently into alpha-aminopropionitrile using cyanide, sulfur reductant, and phosphate-assisted photochemical network chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: This directly incorporates the Sutherland systems-chemistry route in which alanine precursor aminonitrile forms in high yield from acetaldehyde under neutral, UV-driven cyanosulfidic conditions (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson and Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin formation from pyruvate-derived nitrile chemistry

conditions.other: cyanide/ammonia addition to pyruvate followed by CO2 capture to hydantoin scaffold

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes cyanide/ammonia addition and internal cyclization with captured CO2 to produce 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: This follows the recent Bucherer-Bergs prebiotic framing in which pyruvate, cyanide, ammonia, and captured carbon dioxide generate 5-methylhydantoin, linking amino acid and heterocycle chemistry (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine
reactions.mechanism: Ring opening hydrolysis of the hydantoin gives N-carbamoyl alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoyl alanine is part of the experimentally supported Bucherer-Bergs alanine route at elevated temperature (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to DL-alanine

conditions.other: continued hydrolysis after carbamoyl intermediate formation

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis removes the carbamoyl functionality to furnish racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: This completes the Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin route experimentally described for alanine synthesis (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN under concentrated eutectic or evaporitic conditions

conditions.catalyst: concentration on mineral surfaces rather than specific covalent catalysis
conditions.other: requires concentrated HCN, above about 0.01 M; freezing or evaporation concentrates cyanide

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes through dimeric and trimeric nitrile intermediates to diaminomaleonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is the established HCN tetramer and a major branch-point in prebiotic chemistry, formed only after strong concentration of HCN, such as in eutectic freezing or evaporitic settings (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomeric material to mixed amino acids including alanine

conditions.other: represents hydrolysis of DAMN-derived HCN oligomer pool/black polymer rather than clean single-product conversion

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN-derived polymeric material hydrolyzes to a mixed amino-acid distribution in which alanine is one detected product.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports alanine formation from hydrolysis of concentrated HCN oligomer/polymer material, but not as a clean selective DAMN-only transformation; this node is therefore intentionally represented as a low-specificity contributory source of DL-alanine (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fe/Ni sulfide synthesis of mixed amino acids including alanine

conditions.other: 10 day hydrothermal incubation; complex mixture-producing process

reactions.mechanism: Hydrothermal carbonyl/cyanide/sulfur chemistry on Fe/Ni sulfides produces a mixture of organics including alpha-amino acids such as alanine.
reactions.reasoning: This is included as a coarse-grained literature-supported route because alanine was detected among products of hydrothermal Fe/Ni sulfide chemistry, though the mechanism is less resolved and less directed than Strecker or pyruvate routes (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 2006).

reactions.name: Interstellar ice photolysis delivery route to alanine precursor pool

conditions.temperature: cryogenic formation then surface warming to 300K after delivery
conditions.catalyst: UV photolysis and mineral stabilization after deposition
conditions.other: represents exogenous photochemical source from H2O:CH3OH:NH3:HCN-like ice chemistry; alanine formed at low yield relative to glycine

reactions.mechanism: UV photolysis of interstellar ice analogues forms amino acids including alanine, which can be delivered to surface reservoirs and merged into terrestrial pools.
reactions.reasoning: The review specifically identifies UV photolysis of ice analogs as an alanine source, albeit low-yield and racemic. This is retained as a distinct route feeding the same DL-alanine pool (Bernstein et al., 2002; Muñoz Caro et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Biochemical network-level symmetry breaking from DL-alanine pool to L-alanine

conditions.other: coupled to terrestrial symmetry-breaking, likely indirect via homochiral RNA/CISS/autocatalytic selection rather than direct alanine-selective synthesis

reactions.mechanism: A pre-existing racemic alanine pool becomes L-enriched through systems-level selection and amplification in a broader chiral network, potentially downstream of magnetite/CISS-enabled homochiral nucleotide chemistry and stereoselective peptide selection.
reactions.reasoning: The literature strongly rejects a validated direct abiotic route to enantiopure L-alanine and instead points to indirect terrestrial symmetry breaking, potentially involving CISS-driven magnetite effects on nucleotide precursors, homochiral RNA emergence, and later stereoselective peptide/amino-acid selection. This step is therefore explicitly speculative but necessary to connect the experimentally supported DL-alanine network to the requested target (Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk et al., 2023; Ozturk et al., 2025; Blackmond et al., 2025).

pathways.name: Classic Miller-Strecker route
pathways.description: Spark discharge in a reducing atmosphere produces HCN and acetaldehyde, which undergo canonical Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis to DL-alanine; later biochemical symmetry breaking yields L-alanine.

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S Miller-Strecker route
pathways.description: Sulfur-rich volcanic spark chemistry produces the same Strecker feedstocks with enhanced alanine productivity, converging through alpha-aminopropionitrile and DL-alanine.

pathways.name: Classic Miller plus cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker route
pathways.description: Spark-produced HCN and acetaldehyde are funneled into the higher-yield cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker branch to alpha-aminopropionitrile, then to DL-alanine and finally L-enrichment.

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S plus cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker route
pathways.description: H2S-rich spark chemistry supplies HCN and acetaldehyde that enter a surface cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker regime, giving aminonitrile and then alanine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyrite-coupled pyruvate reductive amination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction produces pyruvate, which undergoes iron-sulfur reductive amination to DL-alanine before later L-selection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal mixed-valence iron reductive amination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate formed from CO2 is aminated by mixed-valence iron mineral chemistry under alkaline vent-like conditions.

pathways.name: Ni/H2 reductive amination route
pathways.description: Pyruvate generated hydrothermally is converted under mild Ni/H2 conditions to DL-alanine, linking geochemical carbon fixation to a biosynthetically analogous prebiotic step.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate enters surface cyanide/ammonia/CO2 chemistry to form 5-methylhydantoin, then N-carbamoyl alanine, then DL-alanine and later L-enrichment.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Surface-concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN and related polymers that hydrolyze to a mixed amino-acid pool containing DL-alanine, then feed L-selection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Fe/Ni sulfide direct mixed-product route
pathways.description: Vent-like Fe/Ni sulfide chemistry directly generates alanine among a mixture of organics from carbonyl/cyanide/sulfur feedstocks, feeding the racemic alanine pool.
